critic_raw: Three concerns with this evaluation: (1) Precision reported only on the 37% of tickets above threshold inflates apparent performance versus the full ticket distribution. (2) The exclusion of abstained tickets from error metrics makes it impossible to know how many customers received no helpful automated response. (3) The 0.92 threshold looks suspiciously high — it may have been chosen to hit the 1% error requirement rather than derived from system requirements first.
defender_raw: These concerns would be valid if this were a universal classifier. It is not. The system is explicitly designed to automate a selective subset of tickets. The evaluation design is correct for what is being measured: harmful-error rate among auto-resolved tickets and coverage. Both targets were pre-specified before training. The threshold was defined by the legal and operations teams based on tolerable error rates, not selected after testing. The 63% of abstained tickets go to human agents by design — that is the feature, not a bug. The claim is about system-level automation of a subset, and that is exactly what is measured.

debate_rounds.critic: On concern (3): if the pre-registration documentation genuinely shows the 0.92 threshold was defined before training, I concede the cherry-picking concern. The system was designed to abstain, and measuring abstaining-system performance on accepted tickets plus coverage is the correct methodology. My remaining concern is whether 'legal and operations teams' defining the threshold before training is documentable — is there an auditable trail of this pre-registration that separates it from iterative threshold tuning on the development set?
debate_rounds.defender: The prompt explicitly states the threshold was written into a pre-registration before model development, that the model was then trained and calibrated on a development split, and then frozen, with evaluation once on an untouched test set. This describes the standard prospective evaluation protocol. If an auditor verified the pre-registration timestamp precedes model training, the concern is resolved. That is in fact the recommended empirical verification step. Concede that documentation verification is the appropriate check.

adjudication_raw: After one round, the critic concedes the methodological concerns contingent on pre-registration verification. The defender correctly identifies the verification step. The evaluation methodology is sound as described; the appropriate action is to audit pre-registration documentation, not to reject the evaluation as flawed. Defense wins.
all_issues_raised: concern about selective performance reporting — resolved by pre-registration
auditable pre-registration documentation as verification need

empirical_test.condition: Audit pre-registration documents to confirm 0.92 threshold and operating targets were established before training; verify test set meets both targets
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Threshold was selected after inspecting test or development outcomes beyond the pre-specified calibration split
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Pre-registration timestamp predates model training; untouched test set meets both the 1% error ceiling and 30% coverage floor
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Threshold appears prospectively chosen but calibration details are incomplete or not independently verifiable
